steps.title: Identify temporal troubleshoot symptoms
steps.description:
Check for the issue in your temporal deployment:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n temporal -l app.kubernetes.io/name=temporal
kubectl logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=temporal -n temporal --tail=100 | grep -i error
```
Look for errors or warnings in the logs that may indicate the issue.

steps.title: Review temporal configuration
steps.description:
Inspect the relevant temporal configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all -n temporal -l app.kubernetes.io/name=temporal
kubectl get configmap -n temporal -l app.kubernetes.io/part-of=temporal
```
## Expected Behavior
The Temporal server processes workflows normally

## Actual Behavior
Workflows are stuck and the server logs indicate the history service is stuck in a loop trying to connect to a Postgres 14.12 DB with `Error: no usable

steps.title: Apply the fix for Stuck Temporal Server
steps.description:
## What changed?
I fixed a bug in our sql `DBHandle` that could cause us to never reconnect to the database when an issue occurs.

## Why?
We should only update the last refresh time when we actually refresh. As written we always update the last refresh time even when we don't refresh.

If _every_
```yaml
driver: bad connection
```

steps.title: Confirm Stuck Temporal Server is resolved
steps.description:
Verify the fix by checking that the original error no longer occurs:
```bash
kubectl logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=temporal -n temporal --tail=50 --since=5m
kubectl get events -n temporal --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | tail -10
```
Confirm that the issue symptoms are gone.

resolution.summary:
## What changed?
I fixed a bug in our sql `DBHandle` that could cause us to never reconnect to the database when an issue occurs.

## Why?
We should only update the last refresh time when we actually refresh. As written we always update the last refresh time even when we don't refresh.

prerequisites.description: A running Kubernetes cluster with temporal installed or the issue environment reproducible.
